message.content: I want to add a `filter` subcommand to TaskTracker. It should support filtering by text query, tag, priority level, and optionally show completed tasks.

content.text: That's a great feature to add. Let me explore the current codebase structure to understand how existing commands are organized, then I'll implement the filter subcommand step by step.

message.content: The commands are in src/commands/ with one file per command.

content.text: Perfect, I can see the pattern. Each command has its own module file with a `run` function. Let me check the Task model to understand what fields are available for filtering.

message.content: Yes, the Task struct has title, completed, priority, due_date, tags, and created_at fields.

content.text: Great. I'll start by adding the Filter variant to the Commands enum in main.rs. The filter command will accept optional arguments for query text, tag name, minimum priority, and a flag to include completed tasks.

input.new_string:
Delete { id: u32 },
    Filter {
        #[arg(short, long)]
        query: Option<String>,
        #[arg(short, long)]
        tag: Option<String>,
        #[arg(short, long)]
        priority: Option<u8>,
        #[arg(long)]
        show_completed: bool,
    },

message.content: Now add the match arm for Filter in main().

content.text: Adding the match arm that routes to the filter command module. I'll also need to add the module declaration.

input.new_string:
Commands::Delete { id } => commands::delete::run(id),
        Commands::Filter { query, tag, priority, show_completed } => commands::filter::run(query, tag, priority, show_completed),

message.content: Create the filter module now.

content.text: I'll create the filter command module. It loads all tasks from storage, applies the filters in sequence (text query, tag, priority, completion status), and displays matching results with a count summary.

message.content: We need to add `matches_filter` to the Task model too.

content.text: Right, the `matches_filter` method needs to be on the Task struct. It should search both the title and tags for case-insensitive matches. Let me add that implementation.

message.content: Register the module in commands/mod.rs.

content.text: Adding the filter module declaration to the commands module.

input.new_string:
pub mod delete;
pub mod filter;

message.content: Let's try building it.

content.text: Running cargo build to check for compilation errors. There might be some issues with imports or type mismatches that we need to fix.

message.content: There's a compile error about the storage module not being found in filter.rs.

content.text: The storage module path needs to use `crate::storage` since filter.rs is inside the commands submodule. Let me check how other command files import it and fix the path.

message.content: Build again.

content.text: The build succeeds now. No warnings from clippy either. Let me run the existing test suite to make sure the new code doesn't break anything.

message.content: Now let's write tests for the filter command.

content.text: I'll add unit tests for the `matches_filter` method first, then integration tests for the filter subcommand. The unit tests will cover case-insensitive matching, tag matching, and edge cases like empty strings.

message.content: Add integration tests too.

content.text: I'll add integration tests that exercise the filter subcommand end-to-end. These tests will set up sample tasks, run the filter command with various arguments, and verify the output contains the expected results.

message.content: Run all tests.

content.text: Running the full test suite including the new unit tests and integration tests for the filter command.

message.content: All tests pass. Can you also add a --sort flag to the filter command? Sort by priority, date, or title.

content.text: Good idea. I'll add a `--sort` option that accepts `priority`, `date`, or `title` as values. I'll use a clap ValueEnum for the sort options and implement the sorting logic after filtering. Default will be by priority descending.

input.new_string:
show_completed: bool,
        #[arg(short, long, value_enum, default_value = "priority")]
        sort: SortField,
    },

#[derive(Clone, clap::ValueEnum)]
enum SortField {
    Priority,
    Date,
    Title,
}

#[derive(Subcommand)]

message.content: Update the filter module to accept the sort field.

content.text: I'll update the filter module's `run` function to accept a sort parameter and apply sorting to the filtered results. Priority sorts descending (highest first), date sorts ascending (earliest first), and title sorts alphabetically.

input.old_string: pub fn run(query: Option<String>, tag: Option<String>, priority: Option<u8>, show_completed: bool) {
input.new_string: pub fn run(query: Option<String>, tag: Option<String>, priority: Option<u8>, show_completed: bool, sort: crate::SortField) {

message.content: Update the match arm in main too.

content.text: Right, the match arm needs to pass the sort field to the filter command's run function.

input.old_string: Commands::Filter { query, tag, priority, show_completed } => commands::filter::run(query, tag, priority, show_completed),
input.new_string: Commands::Filter { query, tag, priority, show_completed, sort } => commands::filter::run(query, tag, priority, show_completed, sort),

message.content: Build and test again.

content.text: Building and running the full test suite to verify the sort functionality compiles correctly and all existing tests still pass.

message.content: Nice, all passing. Let's format and run clippy one more time before committing.

content.text: Running cargo fmt to ensure consistent formatting, then clippy for lint checks. This catches any style issues or potential bugs before we commit.

input.command: cd /home/alex/projects/tasktracker && cargo fmt && cargo clippy -- -W clippy::all 2>&1

message.content: Commit everything with a descriptive message.

content.text: I'll stage all the changed files and create a commit with a clear description of what was added. The commit covers the filter subcommand, the matches_filter method on Task, unit tests, integration tests, and the sort option.

input.command: cd /home/alex/projects/tasktracker && git commit -m "Add filter subcommand with query, tag, priority filtering and sort options"

message.content: Thanks! The filter command works perfectly.

content.text: You're welcome! The filter subcommand is now fully functional with four filter criteria (text query, tag, minimum priority, and completion status) plus three sort options (priority, date, title). The implementation includes comprehensive unit tests for the matching logic and integration tests for the CLI interface. Let me know if you'd like to add any additional features like date range filtering or output format options.
